nameMeanings.name: Higgins
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó hUiginn ‘descendant of Uiginn’, a byname meaning ‘viking, sea-rover’ (from Old Norse víkingr). Compare McGuigan .

English (Lancashire and Yorkshire): variant of Higgin, itself either a Lancashire variant of Huggin (from a pet form of Hugh ), or a variant of Hickin (from a pet form of Richard ), with genitival or post-medieval excrescent -s. See Hick and compare Higson .

variants.name: Higgens
meanings: English (Kent): variant of   Higgins  .

variants.name: McGuigan
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, Liam, Mairead.
Irish (northern): perhaps an Anglicized form of Gaelic Mag Uiginn ‘son of the viking’ (see Higgins 1).
Irish (northern): Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Guagáin, a shortened form of Mag Eochagáin, son of Eochagán, a diminutive of Eochaidh ‘horseman’. Compare Geoghegan .

variants.name: Haggins
meanings: Scottish variant of Irish   Higgins  .

variants.name: Heggins
meanings: Scottish: variant of   Higgins   1.

variants.name: Kiggins
meanings: Irish (Galway and Mayo) and Manx: shortened Anglicized form (with English excrescent -s) of Gaelic Mag Uiginn ‘son of the Viking’ (see Higgins ).

nameMeanings.name: Fanny
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Pet form of   Frances  , very popular in the 18th and 19th centuries, but now much rarer.

variants.name: Frances
meanings: Feminine form of   Francis .  In the 16th century the two spellings were used indiscriminately for both sexes, the distinction in spelling not being established until the 17th century.

nameMeanings.name: or
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Originally the name of the god of thunder in Norse mythology, Thor (Old Norse Þórr). It was not used as a personal name during the Middle Ages. The modern name is either a late 18th-century revival of the god's name or else a vernacular development of Tord .

variants.name: Thor
meanings: Originally the name of the god of thunder in Norse mythology, Thor (Old Norse Þórr). It was not used as a personal name during the Middle Ages. The modern name is either a late 18th-century revival of the god's name or else a vernacular development of Tord .

nameMeanings.name: Edward
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From an Old English personal name derived from ēad ‘prosperity, riches’ + weard ‘guard’. This has been one of the most successful of all Old English names, in frequent use from before the Conquest to the present day, and even being exported into other European languages. It was the name of three Anglo-Saxon kings and has been borne by eight kings of England since the Norman Conquest. It is also the name of the youngest son of Queen Elizabeth II . The most influential early bearer was King Edward the Confessor ( ?1002–66 ; ruled 1042–66 ). In a troubled period of English history, he contrived to rule fairly and (for a time at any rate) firmly. But in the latter part of his reign he paid more attention to his religion than to his kingdom. He died childless, and his death sparked off conflicting claims to his throne, which were resolved by the victory of William the Conqueror at the Battle of Hastings. His memory was honoured by Normans and English alike, for his fairness and his piety. Edward's mother was Norman; he had spent part of his youth in Normandy; and William claimed to have been nominated by Edward as his successor. Edward was canonized in the 12th century, and came to be venerated throughout Europe as a model of a Christian king.

variants.name: Edoardo
meanings: From English. See Edward .

variants.name: Eduardo
meanings: From English. See Edward .

variants.name: Eideard
meanings: Gaelic equivalent of Edward .

variants.name: Édouard
meanings: From English. See Edward .

variants.name: Edvard
meanings: form of English Edward .

historicEvents.title: Wycliffe's Bible
historicEvents.description: John Wycliffe, English theologian and professor at the University of Oxford, completed a translation of the Bible from the Latin Vulgate into Middle English. This translation is known as Wycliffe's Bible and was completed around 1382.

en.lifeSummary: When Fanny or Edward Higgins was born about 1375, in England, his father, Roger Higgins, was 22 and his mother, Matilda Saisach, was 19. He married Elizabeth Joan Bedelgate in 1413, in England. He died about 1425, in his hometown, at the age of 52.
